mcp_params.query: repo:django/django TODO FIXME XXX HACK language:python

findings.description: Repository implements comprehensive CI/CD automation with 18 distinct GitHub Actions workflows covering testing, linting, coverage, benchmarks, and quality checks.
findings.evidence: Workflows identified in .github/workflows/: tests.yml, coverage_tests.yml, coverage_comment.yml, linters.yml, selenium.yml, postgis.yml, schedule_tests.yml, check_commit_messages.yml, check_pr_quality.yml, benchmark.yml, docs.yml, python_matrix.yml, check-migrations.yml, labels.yml, new_contributor_pr.yml, screenshots.yml, schedules.yml. Latest commit fa2a3de6 merged 2026-04-23T02:22:55Z showing active CI execution.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current CI/CD practices. Consider adding public status badges to README for transparency.

findings.description: Multi-dimensional test coverage including platform-specific (Windows/Linux), database-specific (PostGIS), browser automation (Selenium), and JavaScript tests.
findings.evidence: tests.yml workflow shows: Windows job with Python 3.14 on windows-latest, javascript-tests job with Node.js 20, scripts-tests job for utility scripts. Separate workflows for selenium.yml, postgis.yml, and coverage_tests.yml demonstrating comprehensive test strategy.
findings.recommendation: Continue comprehensive testing approach. Current coverage is exemplary.

findings.description: Zero technical debt markers (TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK) found in Python codebase, indicating excellent code hygiene and proper issue tracking discipline.
findings.evidence: Search query 'repo:django/django TODO FIXME XXX HACK language:python' returned total_count: 0, incomplete_results: false. This is exceptional for a project of this scale.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current practice of tracking technical debt through Trac issue system rather than code comments.

findings.description: Professional project organization with clear separation of concerns and comprehensive configuration for quality tooling.
findings.evidence: Root structure shows: django/ (source), tests/ (test suite), docs/ (documentation), scripts/ (utilities), js_tests/ (JavaScript tests). Configuration files include: .flake8, pyproject.toml, tox.ini, .pre-commit-config.yaml, .editorconfig, biome.json for JS. Total of 28 items in root tree.
findings.recommendation: Current structure is optimal. Continue maintaining clear boundaries between source, tests, and documentation.

findings.description: Modern dependency management using pyproject.toml with minimal runtime dependencies and proper version constraints.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml shows: requires-python >= 3.12, only 3 core dependencies (asgiref>=3.9.1, sqlparse>=0.5.0, tzdata for Windows), optional dependencies properly scoped (argon2-cffi>=23.1.0, bcrypt>=4.1.1), build-system uses setuptools>=77.0.3. Uses modern PEP 621 standard.
findings.recommendation: Continue minimalist dependency approach. Current dependency hygiene is exemplary.

findings.description: Excellent PR management with no stale PRs and active merge velocity. All open PRs created within last 4 days.
findings.recommendation: Continue current PR review practices. The balance between thorough review and merge velocity is optimal.

findings.description: Rigorous PR quality standards enforced through comprehensive checklist including novel AI assistance disclosure requirement.
findings.evidence: All reviewed PRs include checklist items: contribution guidelines compliance, security disclosure confirmation, branch targeting, commit message format, AI assistance disclosure (required), Trac ticket linking, test coverage requirements, documentation updates. Example from PR #21154: 'AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)' section with checkbox for disclosure and verification.
findings.recommendation: The AI disclosure requirement is innovative and promotes transparency. Consider documenting this as a best practice for other projects.

findings.description: Highly active development with multiple commits per day from diverse contributors.
findings.evidence: Recent commits show: fa2a3de6 (2026-04-23T02:22:55Z by jacobtylerwalls), a586f03f (2026-04-23T02:22:55Z by rodbv/jacobtylerwalls), 61a62be3 (2026-04-22T21:28:35Z by charettes/jacobtylerwalls), 63c56cda (2026-04-22T21:06:29Z by annalauraw/jacobtylerwalls). All commits within last 24-48 hours with different authors showing active collaboration.
findings.recommendation: Activity level is healthy and sustainable. Continue current contribution practices.

findings.description: Multiple automated code quality tools configured and enforced via pre-commit hooks and CI.
findings.evidence: Configuration files present: .flake8 (linting rules), pyproject.toml with [tool.black] target-version py312 and [tool.isort] profile black, .pre-commit-config.yaml for pre-commit hooks, biome.json for JavaScript linting. Workflow linters.yml exists for CI enforcement.
findings.recommendation: Current multi-layered quality enforcement is comprehensive. No changes needed.

findings.description: Dedicated test infrastructure with separate test directory and multiple test requirement files.
findings.evidence: Repository tree shows: tests/ directory at root level, js_tests/ for JavaScript tests. tests.yml references 'tests/requirements/py3.txt' for test dependencies. Multiple specialized test workflows (selenium.yml, postgis.yml, coverage_tests.yml) indicate comprehensive test strategy.
findings.recommendation: Test infrastructure is mature and well-organized. Continue current practices.

findings.description: Django uses Trac (code.djangoproject.com) for issue tracking instead of GitHub Issues. GitHub Issues are disabled with 0 open and 0 closed issues found.
findings.evidence: list_issues returned totalCount:0 for both open and closed states. CONTRIBUTING.rst states: 'Warning: non-trivial pull requests (anything more than fixing a typo) without Trac tickets will be closed! Please file a ticket at https://code.djangoproject.com/newticket. Django uses Trac to keep track of bugs, feature requests, and associated patches because GitHub doesn't provide adequate tooling for its community.'
findings.recommendation: This is a deliberate architectural choice for enterprise-grade issue tracking. Consider adding a CONTRIBUTING note in the GitHub repo README pointing new contributors to the Trac workflow to reduce friction.

findings.description: Excellent contributor diversity with 8 unique authors in the 10 most recent commits, demonstrating very low bus factor risk.
findings.evidence: list_commits (10 most recent, April 2026) shows authors: rodbv (Rodrigo Vieira), charettes (Simon Charette), annalauraw (Annabelle Wiegart), DineshThumma9 (Dinesh), aadeina (Amar), Artyom Kotovskiy, cliffordgama (Clifford Gama), felixxm (Mariusz Felisiak). Committers include jacobtylerwalls (Jacob Walls), sarahboyce (Sarah Boyce), and web-flow, showing active maintainer rotation.
findings.recommendation: Continue current practices. The distributed maintainership model is excellent.

findings.description: All commits follow rigorous review practices with Trac ticket references and co-author attribution, indicating strong code quality culture.
findings.evidence: Commit messages include: 'Fixed #10919 -- Added delete_confirmation_max_display to ModelAdmin', 'Fixed #37057 -- Adjusted UniqueConstraint handling', 'Fixed #35870 -- Made blank choice label in forms more accessible. Co-Authored-By: Marijke Luttekes'. All commits reference Trac tickets and thank reviewers/reporters by name.
findings.recommendation: Maintain this exemplary commit message discipline as it ensures traceability and acknowledges contributor efforts.

findings.description: Comprehensive governance documentation present including CONTRIBUTING.rst, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, and extensive AUTHORS file (45,163 bytes).
findings.evidence: get_file_contents on CONTRIBUTING.rst shows extensive contribution guidelines pointing to docs/internals/contributing/ and online documentation at https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/. search_code found .github/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md. File tree shows AUTHORS file at 45,163 bytes. CONTRIBUTING.rst states: 'As a contributor, you can help us keep the Django community open and inclusive. Please read and follow our Code of Conduct at https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/.'
findings.recommendation: Governance is exemplary. Consider adding a GOVERNANCE.md file to explicitly document the Django Software Foundation's role and decision-making processes for GitHub-native contributors.

findings.description: GitHub-based discussion metrics unavailable as Django community engagement occurs primarily through Trac, mailing lists, and forums rather than GitHub Issues or Discussions.
findings.evidence: list_issues returned 0 results for both open and closed states. CONTRIBUTING.rst explicitly directs contributors to Trac. No GitHub Discussions feature detected in repository structure.
findings.recommendation: Consider enabling GitHub Discussions as a supplementary channel for Q&A and feature discussions to lower barriers for GitHub-native developers while maintaining Trac for formal bug tracking.

findings.description: Active and rigorous release management with comprehensive changelog documentation and regular patch releases.
findings.evidence: search_code for docs/releases/6.0 found 6 files: 6.0.txt, 6.0.1.txt, 6.0.2.txt, 6.0.3.txt, 6.0.4.txt, 6.0.5.txt, indicating at least 5 patch releases for version 6.0 series. list_commits shows daily commit activity with latest commit fa2a3de6ede10b005fc2c1d23f4cffb53eaec425 dated 2026-04-23T02:22:55Z.
findings.recommendation: Continue current release practices. The detailed release notes and regular patch cadence demonstrate excellent maintenance discipline.

findings.description: Strong culture of contributor attribution with co-author tags, reviewer acknowledgments, and comprehensive AUTHORS file.
findings.evidence: Commit messages consistently include 'Thanks [name] for the review and guidance' and 'Co-authored-by' tags. Examples: 'Thanks Jacob Tyler Walls for the review and guidance, Tobias McNulty for the report, and terminator14 for the solution suggested' (commit fa2a3de6). AUTHORS file is 45,163 bytes indicating extensive contributor recognition.
findings.recommendation: This is a best practice that fosters community engagement. Continue and document this practice in contribution guidelines.

findings.description: Professional development tooling in place including pre-commit hooks, linting, testing infrastructure, and CI/CD configuration.
findings.evidence: get_repository_tree shows: .pre-commit-config.yaml (797 bytes), .flake8 (297 bytes), tox.ini (2,195 bytes), biome.json (1,699 bytes), .readthedocs.yml (410 bytes), .github directory, tests directory, indicating comprehensive quality assurance infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Infrastructure is excellent. Ensure new contributors are aware of pre-commit hook setup in onboarding documentation.

findings.description: Excellent README.rst with comprehensive coverage of installation, usage, documentation access, community links, contribution guidelines, and testing instructions.
findings.evidence: README.rst (2,017 bytes) at path 'README.rst'. Contains: installation pointer to 'docs/intro/install.txt', tutorial series references ('docs/intro/tutorial01.txt', 'docs/intro/tutorial02.txt', etc.), deployment guide reference ('docs/howto/deployment/index.txt'), online docs link (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/), community resources (Django Discord, Django Forum), contribution link (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/), testing instructions reference ('docs/internals/contributing/writing-code/unit-tests.txt'), and Django Software Foundation fundraising link.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. README is exemplary and follows best practices for open-source projects.

findings.description: World-class documentation infrastructure with comprehensive Sphinx-based documentation covering tutorials, API reference, topical guides, and how-to guides.
findings.evidence: docs/ directory tree contains: 'docs/ref/' (API reference), 'docs/topics/' (topical guides), 'docs/howto/' (how-to guides), 'docs/intro/' (tutorials with 8 parts: tutorial01.txt through tutorial08.txt totaling >145KB), 'docs/internals/' (internals documentation), 'docs/releases/' (release notes), 'docs/faq/', 'docs/glossary.txt', 'docs/conf.py' (Sphinx configuration), 'docs/Makefile', 'docs/make.bat', 'docs/_theme/' (custom theme), '.readthedocs.yml' (ReadTheDocs configuration). Tutorial files observed: tutorial01.txt (10,844 bytes), tutorial02.txt (26,180 bytes), tutorial03.txt (18,075 bytes), tutorial04.txt (14,218 bytes), tutorial05.txt (28,460 bytes), tutorial06.txt (5,508 bytes), tutorial07.txt (16,289 bytes), tutorial08.txt (4,707 bytes). Additional intro docs: contributing.txt (23,718 bytes), overview.txt (12,922 bytes), reusable-apps.txt (14,368 bytes), install.txt (2,808 bytes), whatsnext.txt (9,095 bytes).
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Documentation infrastructure is industry-leading with professional build tools, versioning, and hosting at https://docs.djangoproject.com.

findings.description: Exceptional release documentation with 586+ individual release note files spanning from version 0.95 to 6.1+, covering 19+ years of development history.
findings.evidence: docs/releases/ directory contains 586+ files including: major releases (1.0.txt, 2.0.txt, 3.0.txt, 4.0.txt, 5.0.txt, 6.0.txt), minor releases (4.2.30.txt, 5.1.15.txt, 6.0.5.txt), patch releases for all versions, index.txt (6,172 bytes) for navigation, and security.txt (70,747 bytes) dedicated to security advisories. Examples of release files: 1.0.txt (10,190 bytes), 4.2.txt (22,377 bytes), 5.0.txt (26,986 bytes), 6.0.txt (25,030 bytes), showing detailed feature documentation for major releases.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. This is the gold standard for changelog documentation in open-source projects.

findings.description: Comprehensive contribution documentation with clear workflows, tooling requirements, and governance policies including a Code of Conduct.
findings.evidence: CONTRIBUTING.rst (1,115 bytes) at root level states: 'Examples of contributions include: Code patches, Documentation improvements, Bug reports and patch reviews. Extensive contribution guidelines are available in the repository at docs/internals/contributing/, or online at: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/. Warning: non-trivial pull requests (anything more than fixing a typo) without Trac tickets will be closed! Please file a ticket to suggest changes. Django uses Trac to keep track of bugs, feature requests, and associated patches. Code of Conduct: Please read and follow our Code of Conduct (https://www.djangoproject.com/conduct/).' Additionally, AUTHORS file (45,163 bytes) lists all contributors, and docs/intro/contributing.txt (23,718 bytes) provides detailed tutorial for new contributors.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Contribution guidelines are clear, comprehensive, and properly communicate the Trac-based workflow that differs from typical GitHub-only projects.

findings.description: Extensive tutorial series with 8 progressive tutorials plus additional guides covering quick starts, overviews, and specialized topics. Visual aids included.
findings.evidence: docs/intro/ directory contains: tutorial01.txt through tutorial08.txt (8 tutorials totaling >145KB of content), overview.txt (12,922 bytes quick start guide), reusable-apps.txt (14,368 bytes packaging tutorial), whatsnext.txt (9,095 bytes next steps guide), contributing.txt (23,718 bytes contribution tutorial), install.txt (2,808 bytes installation guide), and _images/ directory for tutorial screenshots. Tutorial progression covers: getting started, database/admin, views, forms, testing, static files, admin customization, and third-party packages.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Tutorial quality is renowned in the Python community and serves as a model for other projects.

findings.description: Security policy is present and references Django's official security disclosure program at djangoproject.com/security. This demonstrates a mature, well-established responsible disclosure process.
findings.evidence: File .github/SECURITY.md exists with content: 'Please see https://www.djangoproject.com/security/'. Retrieved via get_file_contents tool.
findings.recommendation: Current policy is adequate. Consider adding a brief summary of the reporting process directly in SECURITY.md for quick reference.

findings.description: No dedicated Python security-focused static analysis tool (CodeQL, Bandit, Semgrep) is configured in CI workflows. While code quality linters (flake8, black, isort) are present, they do not perform security vulnerability detection.
findings.evidence: Search query 'repo:django/django filename:codeql path:.github/workflows' returned 0 results. Workflow file .github/workflows/linters.yml contains flake8, black, isort, biome, and zizmor, but no SAST security scanners for Python code.
findings.recommendation: Enable GitHub CodeQL for Python or integrate Bandit/Semgrep in CI to automatically detect security vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, XSS, insecure deserialization, and other OWASP risks.

findings.description: Excellent supply-chain security practices observed: GitHub Actions are SHA-pinned, minimal permissions model is enforced (contents: read), and zizmor security scanner is actively used to detect workflow vulnerabilities.
findings.evidence: File .github/workflows/linters.yml shows: liskin/gh-problem-matcher-wrap@e7b7beaaafa52524748b31a381160759d68d61fb (SHA-pinned), zizmorcore/zizmor-action@e639db99335bc9038abc0e066dfcd72e23d26fb4 (SHA-pinned), permissions: contents: read, persist-credentials: false in checkout steps.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current practices. This is a security strength demonstrating mature DevSecOps awareness.

findings.description: No observable Dependabot or Renovate configuration file exists. While dependency scanning may be enabled via GitHub UI, there is no infrastructure-as-code evidence of automated dependency vulnerability management.
findings.evidence: Attempted get_file_contents on .github/dependabot.yml returned error: 'Failed to get file contents. The path does not point to a file or directory, or the file does not exist in the repository.'
findings.recommendation: Create .github/dependabot.yml to manage Python and JavaScript dependency updates as code. Configure it to monitor requirements files and package.json for security updates.

findings.description: Minimal use of potentially dangerous shell=True pattern found in codebase. Only 2 instances detected in utility scripts (django/utils/version.py and scripts/manage_translations.py), not in user-facing application code.
findings.evidence: Search query 'repo:django/django shell=True language:python' returned 2 results: django/utils/version.py and scripts/manage_translations.py. These are internal utility scripts, not web-facing code.
findings.recommendation: Current usage appears controlled and acceptable for utility scripts. Continue to avoid shell=True in web-facing code and consider adding linter rules to flag new instances.

findings.description: Repository includes zizmor security scanner specifically for detecting vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions workflows, demonstrating proactive CI/CD security monitoring.
findings.evidence: File .github/workflows/linters.yml contains job 'zizmor' using zizmorcore/zizmor-action@e639db99335bc9038abc0e066dfcd72e23d26fb4 with annotations enabled and advanced-security: false.
findings.recommendation: Continue using zizmor. Consider enabling advanced-security: true if the repository has GitHub Advanced Security license to get enhanced scanning capabilities.

findings.description: CI workflows follow security best practices with minimal permission grants and disabled credential persistence, reducing attack surface for compromised workflows.
findings.evidence: File .github/workflows/linters.yml shows 'permissions: contents: read' at workflow level and 'persist-credentials: false' in all checkout actions.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current practices. This follows GitHub security best practices for workflow hardening.

findings.description: Cannot verify if GitHub secret scanning is enabled or if there are active secret scanning alerts due to API permission restrictions on public repositories. No public evidence of secret scanning configuration.
findings.evidence: API endpoint list_secret_scanning_alerts requires admin permissions on public repositories (would return 403 Forbidden). No alternative public signals available to confirm secret scanning status.
findings.recommendation: Ensure GitHub secret scanning is enabled via repository security settings. Consider using git-secrets or similar tools in pre-commit hooks as an additional layer.

findings.description: Repository contains a comprehensive LICENSE file with complete BSD-3-Clause license text. Copyright holder is clearly identified as 'Django Software Foundation and individual contributors'.
findings.evidence: LICENSE file (SHA: 5f4f225dd282aa7e4361ec3c2750bbbaaed8ab1f) contains full BSD-3-Clause text: 'Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation and individual contributors. All rights reserved. Redistribution and use in source and binary forms, with or without modification, are permitted provided that the following conditions are met: 1. Redistributions of source code must retain the above copyright notice... 2. Redistributions in binary form... 3. Neither the name of Django nor the names of its contributors...'
findings.recommendation: No action required. License file is complete and legally sound.

findings.description: License is properly declared in pyproject.toml using SPDX identifier BSD-3-Clause, compliant with PEP 639 modern Python packaging standards. All license files are explicitly declared.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml (SHA: c64cd28dab46fc6f1a24ef3e963cd6bd54186c93) contains: 'license = "BSD-3-Clause"' and 'license-files = ["LICENSE", "LICENSE.python", "AUTHORS"]' under [project] section.
findings.recommendation: No action required. Metadata is fully compliant with modern Python packaging standards.

findings.description: Repository includes LICENSE.python file for proper attribution of Python Software Foundation code incorporated into Django. AUTHORS file (45KB) provides comprehensive contributor acknowledgment.
findings.evidence: Repository tree shows presence of 'LICENSE.python' (blob SHA: 2fc28e876fa1fc8c536f46b81789e2bb51b323c0, size: 14256 bytes) and 'AUTHORS' (blob SHA: 07c87068a9d3b46491391818053839dcd5ffcf1e, size: 45163 bytes). pyproject.toml declares both in license-files array.
findings.recommendation: No action required. Demonstrates exemplary third-party attribution practices.

findings.description: Source files do not contain license headers. Search for 'Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation' in Python files returned zero results. Sample file django/__init__.py (SHA: 4402600c576fab7d8e88389799558aa963369b2d) contains no license header.
findings.evidence: Code search query 'repo:django/django "Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation" language:python' returned: total_count: 0, incomplete_results: false. File django/__init__.py content shows no copyright header, starts directly with: 'from django.utils.version import get_version'
findings.recommendation: Consider adding brief license headers to source files for defense-in-depth, though not required for BSD-3-Clause. Example: '# Copyright (c) Django Software Foundation and individual contributors. # See LICENSE file for full text.'

findings.description: All declared dependencies use permissive licenses compatible with BSD-3-Clause. Core dependencies are asgiref (BSD), sqlparse (BSD), and tzdata (Public Domain/Apache 2.0). Optional dependencies argon2-cffi (MIT) and bcrypt (Apache 2.0) are also compatible.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml dependencies section lists: 'asgiref>=3.9.1', 'sqlparse>=0.5.0', 'tzdata; sys_platform == "win32"' under dependencies, and 'argon2-cffi>=23.1.0', 'bcrypt>=4.1.1' under optional-dependencies. All use permissive licenses with no copyleft obligations.
findings.recommendation: No action required. Dependency license stack is clean and compatible.

findings.description: License files are properly configured for distribution in Python packages. The license-files declaration ensures LICENSE, LICENSE.python, and AUTHORS are included in sdist and wheel distributions.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml [project] section declares 'license-files = ["LICENSE", "LICENSE.python", "AUTHORS"]' ensuring these files are packaged with distributions per PEP 639.
findings.recommendation: No action required. Distribution configuration is compliant.
